question: Design a `Book` class with private members `title`, `author`, and `isbn` (all strings). Provide public methods to set and get these values, ensuring basic encapsulation.

test_cases.input:
Set values for 1984 by George Orwell.
Update title to Animal Farm.
test_cases.expected:
Book Details:
Title: 1984
Author: George Orwell
ISBN: 978-0451524935

Updated Title: Animal Farm

errors: The setter methods (e.g., updateBookTitle) and constructor parameters pass `std::string` by value instead of by `const std::string&`. This creates an unnecessary copy of the string object for each call, leading to reduced efficiency, especially with large strings or frequent calls.

The getter methods (e.g., retrieveBookTitle) return `std::string` by value, which creates a copy of the internal string. While sometimes acceptable, returning `const std::string&` can be more efficient if the caller doesn't need a mutable copy.
The member variables and method names are overly verbose (e.g., `theBookTitle`, `updateBookTitle`, `retrieveBookTitle`), which can hinder readability without adding significant clarity.

Getter methods are not `const`-qualified (e.g., `std::string retrieveBookTitle()`). This is a readability and robustness issue, as it doesn't clearly indicate that the method does not modify the object's state, and prevents calling them on `const` Book objects.
